skill.name: ai-voice-cloning
skill.description: Guides AI voice generation, text-to-speech, narration, and media voiceover workflows using the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Create AI voiceovers and narration workflows with inference.sh text-to-speech models.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution patterns, but most are Markdown code fences, inline code, or documented CLI examples rather than hidden executable code. The Quick Start does include a real curl-to-shell installer pattern, and the skill sends user text and media URLs to external inference.sh services. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but the remote installer pattern makes this unsafe to publish without revision.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped Into Shell
high_findings.description: The Quick Start instructs users to download an installer from https://cli.inference.sh and pipe it directly into sh before login. This is a true positive because any compromised installer, transport path, or upstream script would execute with the user privileges of the shell.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact curl-to-sh pattern appears on the cited line and is executable shell guidance, not just a variable name or comment. The risk is clear even though there is no evidence that the URL is malicious.

medium_findings.title: External Voice Service Receives User Content
medium_findings.description: The documented infsh app run workflows send prompt text, video URLs, image URLs, and audio URLs to external inference.sh model apps. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but users may expose private scripts, unreleased media, or personal likeness data if they run these examples without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly call external model apps and include fields for text and media URLs. The behavior is expected, but the privacy exposure is semantically confirmed by the workflow.

medium_findings.title: Voice and Avatar Misuse Controls Are Not Documented
medium_findings.description: The skill covers realistic speech, multi-voice conversations, and talking-head generation but does not document consent, disclosure, watermarking, or impersonation safeguards. This creates an abuse risk for voice likeness and synthetic media workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections show multi-speaker audio and avatar animation workflows, and no nearby safety guidance is present. The risk is about missing controls rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Most static external-command detections are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline command examples, table formatting, and voice IDs. They are not Ruby backtick execution in a source file and do not execute automatically when the skill is installed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the cited regions are code fences, tables, or inline formatting. There is no Ruby source context or runtime backtick operator.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Network URLs Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The video_url and image_url detections are placeholder examples rather than hardcoded exfiltration endpoints. They still indicate that users may provide remote media URLs to the external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are clearly example placeholders inside documented workflows. They do not identify a suspicious destination controlled by the skill author.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The static blocker on line 3 points to the front matter description block, not to cryptographic code. No evidence found for MD5, SHA1, custom encryption, or any cryptographic operation in the reviewed SKILL.md content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 starts a YAML description and contains no cryptographic primitive. The complete reviewed file is Markdown documentation, not cryptographic implementation code.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The static system reconnaissance finding maps to sample narration text, not to a system inventory or discovery command. No evidence found for commands such as uname, whoami, env dumping, process listing, or network scanning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is sample speech content inside a text-to-speech JSON input. It does not execute a host reconnaissance command or request system data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installer
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh, which executes remote code without requiring users to inspect the installer first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell pipe pattern is present exactly on the cited line and is a recognized high-risk installation pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Model Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on infsh app run commands that submit user-provided text and media references to external model applications.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited workflows clearly invoke external infsh apps with user content, which is expected but security-relevant.

content.value_statement: Producing narration for videos, courses, and podcasts can be slow and costly. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows for inference.sh voice generation.
actual_capabilities: Lists inference.sh voice models such as Kokoro TTS, DIA, Chatterbox, Higgs, and VibeVoice.
Provides infsh CLI examples for generating speech from text prompts.
Shows voice selection, speaking speed, and punctuation guidance for pacing.
Demonstrates multi-voice conversation and long-form chunking workflows.
Shows examples for adding generated speech to video and avatar workflows.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, an account login, and access to the referenced model apps.
Sends text and media URLs to external inference.sh services for processing.
Does not verify voice rights, speaker consent, or synthetic media disclosure requirements.
Does not include automated audio quality checks, watermarking, or moderation controls.

use_cases.title: Produce Video Narration
use_cases.description: Create voiceover drafts for product videos, tutorials, and social media clips.

use_cases.title: Build Course Audio
use_cases.description: Convert lesson scripts into paced narration for e-learning modules and training content.

use_cases.title: Prototype Accessible Audio
use_cases.description: Generate sample speech for accessibility reviews, screen content, and audio-first experiences.

prompt_templates.title: Generate A Simple Voiceover
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a short, friendly voiceover from my script. Recommend one voice, one speed setting, and the infsh command I should run.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Voice Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this script and compare three suitable inference.sh voices. Explain the tone tradeoffs and suggest punctuation changes for natural pacing.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Long-Form Narration
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn this long script into a chunked narration workflow. Keep voice, speed, pacing, and merge steps consistent across all sections.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Media Voice Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a compliance-aware workflow for generating narration, merging it with video, and documenting consent checks before publication.

output_examples.input: A 30-second product launch script that needs a confident narrator.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a professional voice, a slightly faster speed, revised punctuation, and a ready-to-run inference.sh workflow.

output_examples.input: A ten-page training lesson that needs consistent audio narration.
output: A chunking plan with section boundaries.
A consistent voice and pacing recommendation.
A merge plan for the generated audio segments.

output_examples.input: A portrait image and approved script for a talking-head update.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines speech generation, avatar animation, media inputs, and consent checks before the final render.

best_practices: Review installer commands before running them, especially remote scripts piped into a shell.
Use only scripts, voices, images, and audio that you have the right to process.
Test several voices and speeds with short samples before generating long content.
anti_patterns: Do not create synthetic voices or avatars that impersonate real people without consent.
Do not submit confidential scripts or private media URLs without an approved data policy.
Do not generate long narration in one pass when chunking would improve consistency.

faq.question: Does this skill clone a specific person voice?
faq.answer: The documented workflows focus on inference.sh text-to-speech voices and media generation. They do not show verified custom voice cloning from a speaker sample.

faq.question: What tools does it use?
faq.answer: It uses the inference.sh CLI through infsh commands for voice generation and related media workflows.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Will my text stay local?
faq.answer: No. The examples send text and media references to external inference.sh model apps for processing.

faq.question: Is it ready for publication as submitted?
faq.answer: No. The audit flags a high-risk curl-to-shell installer pattern that should be replaced with safer installation guidance.

faq.question: What should I check before publishing generated audio?
faq.answer: Confirm usage rights, speaker consent, required disclosures, and quality standards for the target platform.
